subject: Effect

subject: Attack

subject: Ability

subject: Effect
raw: PERMANENT -1 COST PER RANK A continuous effect with this flaw becomes permanent in duration.

subject: Modifier
raw: A Quirk is generally worth, at most, 2–3 power points, and many are simply 1-point flaws.

subject: Power
raw: On the other hand, a shapeshifter unable to change color (losing some of the power’s utility), or a telepath unable to lie while using Mental Communication, do have Quirks to their powers.

subject: Effect

subject: Effect
raw: Some effects have their range determined by rank.

subject: Check
raw: It allows a Will resistance check to overcome the effect, denying you the defense bonus against that op- ponent (and applying this flaw to the effect).

subject: Attack
raw: For example, a Damage effect might involve whirling blades an attacker can avoid with a successful Dodge resistance check, circumventing the need for a Tough- ness check against the damage.

subject: Check
raw: Similarly a Weaken effect based on a poison dart might add a Toughness check to see if the dart penetrates the target’s skin in addition to making the usual Fortitude check against the effect.

subject: Check
raw: Otherwise the effect works normally and the target makes the usual resistance check against it, if any.

subject: Check
raw: This provides a +10 bonus to resistance checks against the effect, but gives others partial concealment from that sense.

subject: Effect
raw: An opponent unable to use a sense (blind, deaf, etc.) is immune to effects dependent on it.

subject: Effect
raw: To give a target additional resis- tance to a sensory effect, use the Resistible flaw.

subject: Effect
raw: -1 OR -2 COST PER RANK Failing to successfully use an effect with this flaw causes some problematic effect.

subject: Check
raw: Failure includes missing an at- tack check, or the target successfully resisting the effect.

subject: Effect
raw: If the side effect always occurs when you use the effect, whether you succeed or fail, it is worth –2 cost per rank.

subject: Effect
raw: Typical side effects include Affliction, Damage, or Weaken, or the base effect itself (it essentially rebounds and affects you instead).

subject: Attack
raw: The Side Effect does not require an attack check and only affects you, although the GM may permit some Side Effects with the Area modi- fier on a case-by-case basis.

subject: Effect
raw: TIRING -1 COST PER RANK An effect with this flaw causes you to suffer a level of fa- tigue when you use it.

subject: Modifier
raw: In essence, the power requires extra effort in order to use it (see Extra Effort, page 19).

subject: Effect
raw: This makes Tiring a useful flaw for creating an effect you can only use with extra effort.

subject: Effect
raw: For example, a hero might have a rank 12 Damage effect, but routinely use only 8 ranks of it.

subject: Effect
raw: A Tiring effect can be combined with extra effort, but the fatigue stacks, causing a minimum of two levels of fatigue per use.

subject: Effect
raw: -1 COST PER RANK You have no control over an effect with this flaw.

subject: Check
raw: Roll a die each round before you use or maintain the effect.

subject: Ability
raw: Alternately, instead of having a reliability roll, you can choose to have five uses where your effect works normal- ly, then it stops working altogether until you can “recover” it in some way (see the Fades flaw for more on this).

subject: Power
raw: Powers that are only occasionally unreliable (less than about 50% of the time) are better handled as complica- tions (see Complications, page 30).

subject: Advantage
raw: A gun-toting vigilante, Caliber has a variety of guns (acquired via the Equipment advantage).

subject: Effect
raw: Although descriptors don’t always have significant game effects in MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS, they’re perhaps the most important element in providing color and character to the powers of heroes and villains.

subject: Power
raw: Since they involve a change in the sub- ject’s DNA, mutant powers—or at least the potential for them—are inheritable.

subject: Ability
raw: SOURCE A power’s source differs from its origin in that the origin is where the potential or ability to use the power comes from (where the character got the power in the first place), while source is where the power’s effect comes from, or where the power draws its energy.

subject: Power
raw: a dragon’s breath might use magic to power it, or it might be biological, depending on the descriptors applied to it (in other words, how it’s de- fined in terms of the setting).

subject: Power
raw: “Good” and “evil” are common abstract moral sourc- es of powers, but others may include chaos, law, an- archy, order, justice, balance, neutrality, reason, and so forth.

subject: Device
raw: Although tech- nological power sources often involve Devices or Equipment, they don’t necessary have to;

subject: Effect
raw: MEDIUM A power’s medium is what the power uses to accomplish its effect(s).

subject: Effect
raw: a psionic power uses psionic energy to power and accomplish its effects, likewise, a divine power often uses divine energy to power and accomplish its effects.

subject: Effect
raw: For example, the power to throw fireballs granted by the God of Fire is a bestowed origin with a divine source, using fire as the me- dium to cause its Damage effect.

subject: Device
raw: it has an invented origin (someone designed and built it), a techno- logical source (it’s a technological device with a battery), uses a energy medium (an electrical shock), and results in an electrical overload of the tar- get’s nervous system (the result descriptor for its Affliction effect).

subject: Hero
raw: POWERS In many instances, players come up with creative uses for their characters’ descriptors.

subject: Check
raw: So if a fire-using character wants to use a tiny amount of his flame blast power to light some candles, or the electrical-controlling character wants to use some of his power output to act as a living battery to jump-start a car, go for it.

subject: Effect
raw: Creative uses of descriptors with no real game effect are freebies:

subject: Effect
raw: Situations where creative uses of descriptors have a significant game effect can be handled as power stunts:

subject: Effect
raw: pick the effect that best suits the desired outcome and treat it as an Alternate Effect of the power the hero wants to use, with descrip- tors assigned as appropriate.

subject: Attack
raw: If an electrical-controlling hero wants to use his power like a living defibrillator to save a heart-attack victim, for example, that can be a Healing power stunt.

subject: Hero
raw: The hero uses extra effort (and possibly a hero point) and gets a one-shot use of Healing to stabilize the dying victim.

subject: Hero
raw: The GM agrees and so the heroine’s powers have no origin or source descriptors.

subject: Hero
raw: The key tool for handling the ap- plication of descriptors in play is the use of hero points.

subject: Modifier
raw: Temporarily changing a descriptor can be a use of extra ef- fort, like any other power stunt.

subject: Effect
raw: For example, a hero might change the result of an electrical Damage effect to a mag- netic Move Object effect for one use.

subject: Equipment
raw: Villains are forever coming up with doomsday machines and fiendish deathtraps while heroes use all sorts of gear to aid them in their fight for justice.

subject: Device
raw: Many heroes and villains have magical devices such as enchanted weapons and armor, magical talismans, wands and staves of power, and so forth.

subject: Ability
raw: Take away the device, and the wielder loses the ability to use those powers.

subject: Device
raw: Characters who want to have and use a device on a regular basis have to pay power points to have it, just like having any other power.

subject: Device
raw: In other cases, characters may make temporary use of a device.

subject: Device
raw: Most devices are usable by anyone able to operate them, in which case characters may loan devices to each other, or may pick up and use someone else’s device (or even steal a device away from someone in order to use it against them).

subject: Device
raw: Although it has a technological descriptor, the implant can’t be removed without surgery, so it isn’t a device or equipment.

subject: Advantage
raw: Characters with the Inventor and Artificer advantages can create temporary devices for use in an adventure.

subject: Device
raw: Gamemasters may require characters to spend a hero point to make temporary use of a device that doesn’t be- long to them, similar to performing a power stunt without suffering fatigue.

subject: Array
raw: A battlesuit with an array of weapons may have a primary attack effect and several others as Alternate Effects (see the Alternate Effect modifier in the Powers chapter).

subject: Hero
raw: Costumes may have other properties and can even be the source of a hero’s powers, such as in the case of battlesuits (previ- ously).

subject: Advantage
raw: GADGETS & GEAR feature and require characters to pay for it if their charac- ters have such a costume.

subject: Hero
raw: Otherwise characters have to make do with ordinary clothing (which may be damaged or destroyed when they use their powers).

subject: Attack
raw: Magical weap- ons may have greater damage bonuses or grant attack bonuses while magical armor imposes no penalties and provides greater protection.

subject: Attack
raw: A weapon device usually has one or more attack effects but may provide virtually any effect the player wants to include.

subject: Attack
raw: Weapons often have several different attacks as Alternate Effects.

subject: Device
raw: The full range of devices MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS characters can create and use is limited solely by your imagination.

subject: Device
raw: Use the guidelines in this chapter and in the Powers chapter to handle any new devices and their capabilities.

subject: Power
raw: Designing an invention requires an hour’s work per power point of the invention’s cost.

subject: Check
raw: Design Check = DC 10 + invention’s point cost If the check is successful, you have a design for the inven- tion.

subject: Check
raw: For this reason, the GM should make the design check secretly and only inform the player whether or not the character appears to have succeeded.

subject: Power
raw: This requires four hours of work per power point of the invention’s cost, so an invention costing 10 points takes 40 hours (about a week’s work normally, or working two days straight without rest) to construct.

subject: Power
raw: Once the invention is complete, it is good for use in one scene, after which it breaks down or runs out of power.

subject: Hero
raw: The other option is to spend a hero point to get another one-scene use out of the invention.

subject: Check
raw: Each use costs an ad- ditional hero point, but doesn’t require any further skill checks.

subject: Device
raw: Although it’s possible to prepare certain one-use devices in advance, the GM should require the player to spend a hero point to have a particular previously constructed in- vention conveniently on-hand during an adventure.

subject: Check
raw: He rolls a 25 result on the check and, a min- ute later, Makeshift has a makeshift mind-shield he hopes will protect him from Gepetto’s power long enough to try and free his teammates from the vil- lain’s influence.

subject: Check
raw: The inventor makes the check and, if successful, has use of the device for one scene be- fore it burns out, falls apart, blows up, or otherwise fails.

subject: Hero
raw: Inventing mishaps can become a source of adventure ideas and put the heroes in some difficult situations.

subject: Equipment
raw: Just because a character happens to own a cell phone, laptop computer, car, or a home does not mean the char- acter is expected to have ranks in the Equipment advan- tage.

subject: Equipment
raw: Broadly speaking, MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS characters are only expected to pay for adventuring equipment, which is to say items that have a direct impact on their roles as heroes.

subject: Effect
raw: “Jury-rigging” a ritual has the same effects as for an inven- tion.

subject: Check
raw: Spending a hero point allows the ritualist to skip the design check and perform the ritual in a number of rounds equal to its cost.

subject: Check
raw: Magic check against a DC equal of (15 + the ritual’s cost) is needed to successfully perform the ritual.

subject: Device
raw: GADGETS & GEAR EQUIPMENT In addition to their amazing devices, characters often make use of various mundane equipment—ordinary things found in the real world—ranging from a simple set of tools to cell phones, laptop computers, and even common appliances.

subject: Advantage
raw: The character pays the item’s cost out of the points from the Equipment advantage and can thereafter have and use that item.

subject: Advantage
raw: An item’s cost is based on its effects and features, just like a power (see the Powers chapter for more informa- tion), so a ranged weapon has a cost based on its Ranged Damage rank.

subject: Effect
raw: Equipment can have the Alternate Effect modifier (see the Extras section of the Powers chapter), such as a weapon capable of different modes of opera- tion, or a reconfigurable tool.

subject: Array
raw: Characters can also have Alternate Equipment, an array of items usable only one at a time.

subject: Equipment
raw: Alternate Equipment can also include things like an arsenal of weapons the char- acter can swap out, providing different sets of weapons, with only a limited number usable at once.

subject: Equipment
raw: The GM may allow players to spend a hero point in order to have a particular item of equip- ment “on-hand” at a particular time.

subject: Equipment
raw: This is essentially an equipment “power stunt”—a one-time use of the item for one scene—and the Gamemaster rules whether or not having a particular item on-hand is even possible.

subject: Equipment
raw: For example, a hero out for an evening in his secret identity might have something like a concealed weapon or other small item on-hand, but it’s unlikely the character is carry- ing a large weapon or item unless he has some means of concealing it.

subject: Advantage
raw: The Gamemaster may rule some equipment is simply not available or that characters must pay for an additional Feature (or more) in order to have it.

subject: Check
raw: Repairing an item requires a Technology check.

subject: Equipment
raw: AMMO, BATTERIES, AND CHARGES Lots of equipment has a limited lifespan:

subject: Equipment
raw: However, it can be a hassle to keep track of the lifespan of every piece of equipment the heroes may have (to say nothing of all the villains and supporting characters).

subject: Device
raw: The material in this book assumes equipment and devices have effectively unlimited use, except when the GM declares a complication, and that heroes properly maintain, charge, reload, and refuel their gear “off-panel” in between the scenes of an adventure, unless the Gamemaster dictates otherwise.

subject: Defense
raw: Steel cuffs have Toughness 9 and are DC 20 to escape using Sleight of Hand (or Technology to pick the lock).

subject: Equipment
raw: Heroes often use surveillance gear to keep tabs on crimi- nals and their activities.

subject: Check
raw: It requires an Investigation or Perception check against the results of the Sleight of Hand check used to conceal the microphone to find it.

subject: Attack
raw: It requires a successful attack check to plant a tracer on a target (or a Sleight of Hand check to plant it outside of action time).

subject: Condition
raw: Night vision goggles use passive light gathering to improve vision in near-dark conditions.

subject: Attack
raw: These tinted eye-coverings provide pro- tection against blinding light, granting a +5 equipment bonus to resistance checks against visual Dazzle attacks involving bright light.

subject: Array
raw: Some characters may have a Removable array of devices instead, allowing for far more unusual ef- fects than run-of-the-mill equipment.

subject: Equipment
raw: Still, heroes often have 1-point items in their utility belts, like flashlights, re- breathers, and so forth.

subject: Hero
raw: Characters who don’t have any offen- sive powers often rely on weapons to get the job done.

subject: Effect
raw: They typically have a Strength-based Damage effect (see the Damage effect in the Powers chapter), adding the wielder’s Strength rank to the weapon’s damage rank.

subject: Defense
raw: If a wielder exerts Strength greater than the weapon’s Toughness (4 for wooden weapons, 7 or 8 for metal weapons), the weapon breaks when it is used.

subject: Effect
raw: The effect has the normal cost given in the Powers chapter.

subject: Ability
raw: The effect may also have certain descrip- tors, such as bludgeoning or slashing, for defining things like resistance or vulnerability to certain effects.

subject: Equipment
raw: Characters pay this cost from their equipment points to have a weapon of this type as part of their regular equipment.

subject: Device
raw: GADGETS & GEAR 217 Ranged weapons include both thrown and projectile weapons.

subject: Power
raw: Rifles designed for long-range use, typically in conjunction with a powerful scope or targeting system.

subject: Device
raw: A bow-wielding character may have various “trick” ar- rows with different powers, typically handled as devices.

subject: Attack
raw: Boomerang wielders often use this property of the weapon to feint, allowing for an at- tack against a vulnerable target on the return arc on the attacker’s next turn.

subject: Attack
raw: Armors are categorized as archaic (ancient styles of armor like chain- and plate-mail), modern (typi- cally bulletproof composites and synthetics), and shields (requiring some active use to protect against attacks).

subject: Equipment
raw: Characters pay this cost from their equipment points to have the armor of this type as part of their regular equipment.

subject: Device
raw: SUPER-SHIELDS Just as power armor is a device version of otherwise or- dinary equipment armor, some heroes (and, less often, villains) have shield devices providing them with great- er benefits than an ordinary shield.

subject: Effect
raw: A hero able to hurl a shield at foes can even have a Ranged Damage effect with it.

subject: Hero
raw: Sometimes heroes make use of other means to get around.

subject: Effect
raw: Vehicles can even have power effects of their own.

subject: Effect
raw: A vehicle can have power effects of its own, usually re- flecting the vehicle’s systems.

subject: Effect
raw: Vehicle powers have their normal cost for the ve- hicle (meaning they cost one-fifth the normal amount for the vehicle’s owner, since the effects are incorporated into the vehicle and cost equipment points rather than power points).

subject: Equipment
raw: Just like Alternate Equipment, characters may have mul- tiple vehicles.

subject: Hero
raw: The hero’s player can even spend a hero point to pull out a motorcycle, submarine, jet-ski, or whatever other vehicle the hero might have stashed away waiting for the right occasion.

subject: Advantage
raw: Most cars include such standard features as air condition- ing, air bags, antilock brakes, cruise control, keyless entry, and an AM/FM radio with CD player.

subject: Modifier
raw: Luxury vehicles often also include extras such as power seats, leather uphol- stery, and a sunroof.

subject: Hero
raw: GAMEMASTER GUIDELINES Your GM may have particular guidelines for characters in the game, such as not allowing certain powers or concepts or requiring particular descriptors.

subject: Ability
raw: ABILITIES Choose the ability ranks you want your character to have and pay 2 power points for each rank.

subject: Attack
raw: The standard tank has Tough- Impervious ness 12 and comes equipped with a can- non (Ranged Damage 10, Burst Area 6) and a heavy machine gun (Ranged Multiattack Damage 6).

subject: Attack
raw: Fighter jets have machine guns (Ranged Multiattack Damage 6) and air-to-air missiles (Ranged Damage 11, Burst Area 8, Homing 6).

subject: Power
raw: Bombers may have machine guns and missiles, but also have powerful bombs (Burst Area Damage 12 or higher) they can drop on targets.

subject: Equipment
raw: Teams may even pool their equipment points to have a headquarters they share, with the Gamemaster’s approval.

subject: Hero

subject: Advantage
raw: Headquarters have two main traits—Size and Tough- ness—and a number of possible Features.

subject: Attack
raw: Gen- erally, a combat simulator has a suite of equipment that can simulate any appropriate attack effect at a rank up to the HQ power level.

subject: Check
raw: Heroes often have access to special government channels, while a successful Technology skill check (DC 25) can grant a user illegal access to re- stricted systems.

subject: Check
raw: This allows characters to make full use of the Technology skill and the computer can be pro- grammed to handle routine base functions (including monitoring communications channels and controlling defensive systems).

subject: Check
raw: Skill checks to locate the headquarters have their DC increased by +10.

subject: Attack
raw: A defense system can have any attack effect with a cost no greater than twice the HQ power level.

subject: Attack
raw: Their attack bonus is equal to the power level.

subject: Advantage
raw: Headquarters that have no “exterior” structure, such as an extra-dimensional fortress accessed by a magical talisman, do not have this feature, but instead have things like Dimensional Portal, Isolated, Sealed, and the like.

subject: Power
raw: The ef- fect cannot have a total cost greater than twice the HQ power level and cannot exceed the power level limits.

subject: Advantage
raw: An HQ can have surrounding land of one size category larger than the structure at no cost, without having this feature.

subject: Advantage
raw: If the headquarters has features like Defense System and Security System, they also extend over the grounds (with fences, sensors, weapon emplacements, and so forth).

subject: Check
raw: Heroes use holding cells to contain cap- tured villains until they can be turned over to the proper authorities while villains use them to keep heroes pris- oner until they can dispose of them in their latest death- trap.

subject: Equipment
raw: INFIRMARY An infirmary consists of hospital beds and equipment for the full use of the Treatment skill.

subject: Hero
raw: An infirmary can provide treatment for a number of characters equal to the base’s power level at one time and it can be assumed to have the necessary facilities to handle any unusual physiology of the base’s owner(s).

subject: Hero
raw: Characters can use the laboratory to perform research, study unusual phenomena (including many super-powers), and so forth.

subject: Skill
raw: LIBRARY A library allows for use of various Knowledge skills when doing research.

subject: Power
raw: This is usually a number of residents equal to the HQ’s power level comfortably (pos- sibly more, at the GM’s discretion).

subject: Power
raw: This generally lasts for a number of hours equal to the HQ’s power level.

subject: Advantage
raw: The Gamemaster may rule certain projects require a dedicated workshop of their own (which is an addition- al feature).

subject: Equipment
raw: Given the equip- ment point costs of most HQs, team members rarely have to devote more than a single rank of the Equipment ad- vantage to their headquarters, an any excess points may also cover things like team vehicles and equipment (com- mlinks, for example).

subject: Equipment
raw: In the event that a character has more than one headquar- ters, such as hidden bases scattered around the world, the others are treated as Alternate Equipment:

subject: Hero
raw: The extra headquarters are largely a convenience (for heroes visiting other areas) and insurance against the loss of any one HQ.

subject: Device
raw: GADGETS & GEAR SEA-BASE Gamemasters can use the following sample headquarters as ready-made lairs for supervillains while players can use them as bases for their heroes.

subject: Check
raw: Constructs are subject to the same power level limits as other characters and the Gamemaster should require con- structs controlled by the players to observe these limits.

subject: Hero
raw: Non-player character constructs have their power level determined the same as other NPCs.

subject: Rank
raw: Constructs also have either no Intel- lect and Presence ranks or no Strength and Agility ranks.

subject: Ability
raw: Like inanimate objects, constructs have a Toughness rank, which measures their ability to resist damage.

subject: Advantage
raw: A mobile construct can even have the Defensive Roll advantage.

subject: Hero
raw: SKILLS Constructs can have skills just like characters at the same cost.

subject: Advantage
raw: Constructs can have advantages at the same cost as other characters.

subject: Advantage
raw: Some advantages are less useful or even use- less to constructs and, like skills, constructs cannot have advantages requiring abilities they lack.

subject: Effect
raw: POWERS Constructs can have various power effects, like other char- acters.

subject: Ability
raw: Non-intelligent constructs do exactly as they’re told, without creativity or initiative, while intelligent constructs have the ability to interpret and improvise.

subject: Hero
raw: SKILLS Choose the skill ranks you want your character to have and pay 1 power point per 2 total skill ranks.

subject: Advantage
raw: ADVANTAGES Choose the advantages you want your character to have and pay 1 power point per advantage or rank in a advan- tage.

subject: Hero
raw: COMPLICATIONS Choose at least two complications for your hero.

subject: Hero
raw: Once your GM has ap- proved, your new hero is ready for play!

subject: Ability
raw: Each ability, skill, advantage, power, and other trait has an associated power point cost.

subject: Power
raw: Once spent, power points cannot be re-allo- cated without the use of a particular power or the Game- master’s permission.

subject: Check
raw: • Make any necessary resistance checks to recover You can use extra effort in order to take an additional standard or move action on your turn (see Extra Effort on page 19).

subject: Check
raw: It can involve several checks of the same trait, or multiple traits, such as a Persuasion check to get the heroes on good terms with the King of Atlantis, an Expertise check to explain the particular dan- ger the kingdom faces, and an Insight check to realize the king’s advisor has something other than the best interests of the kingdom and royal family at heart.

subject: Advantage
raw: Sometimes the environment itself can be a danger, particularly when villains try to use it to their advantage.

subject: Check
raw: Fatigued characters who fail a check become exhausted, then incapacitated, at which point the charac- ter’s condition becomes dying after another failed Forti- tude check.

subject: Check
raw: How often characters have to make Fortitude checks de- pends on the conditions.

subject: Hero
raw: Characters can hold their breath for ten rounds (one min- ute) plus a number of rounds equal to twice their Stamina.

subject: Check
raw: Failure on the Fortitude check means the character becomes incapaci- tated .

subject: Check
raw: Catching a falling person or object requires a Dexterity check (DC 5).

subject: Effect
raw: Poisons generally have one of several effects particularly Afflic- tion, Damage, or Weaken.

subject: Check
raw: A Treatment skill check can provide a bonus on resistance checks against poison.

subject: Check
raw: A Treatment skill check can provide a bonus on resistance checks against disease.

subject: Power
raw: RADIATION Radiation in the comic books often causes mutations or triggers latent powers in those exposed to it rather than simply causing radiation sickness.

subject: Check
raw: The victim makes an initial Fortitude check against (DC 10 + radiation’s rank) and an additional check each day.

subject: Effect
raw: Radiation sickness is typically a Weaken Stamina effect, but may include other effects, includ- 238

subject: Condition
raw: Affliction conditions typically include impaired and disabled, perhaps also dazed and stunned for especially severe nausea.

subject: Condition
raw: Common Affliction conditions include impaired and disabled, although a large enough dose or repeated exposure can also Weaken Stamina, even leading to death.

subject: Effect
raw: Typically a Weaken effect against Strength, Agility, or Stamina (sometimes more than one), with Weaken Stamina potentially lethal, if the victim’s Stamina drops below –5.

subject: Condition
raw: Side-effects might include conditions like dazed, impaired, or hindered.

subject: Effect
raw: Heroes able to ignore the effects of deep space must have Immunity to suffocation, vacuum, and radiation, at a mini- mum.

subject: Attack
raw: If your result equals or exceeds the target’s defense, your attack hits and may have some effect.

subject: Attack
